title: Outside Unit Not Running | Tampa, FL
hero: Outdoor dead while indoor runs is almost always permission: 24 V call, high-voltage through disconnect/contactor, or motor/compressor electrical—not ‘low refrigerant’ first.
summary_30s: Prove Y/O at the outdoor low-voltage, then line voltage out of the disconnect, then contactor pull and capacitor-assisted fan start. Tampa heat and ants in contactors make the electrical branch statistically dominant.

quick_checks.label: Audible and visual
quick_checks.action: Outdoor fan blade stationary; contactor pulled or not; any hum.
quick_checks.risk_if_ignored: Assuming compressor failure without electrical map wastes replacements.

quick_checks.label: Disconnect
quick_checks.action: Fully seated; no melted holder; correct orientation.
quick_checks.risk_if_ignored: Partial insert can read as random outdoor death.

quick_checks.label: Breaker
quick_checks.action: HVAC breaker position; one reset only if no odor/heat.
quick_checks.risk_if_ignored: Repeated resets on ground fault arc-damages equipment.

quick_checks.label: Contactor manually
quick_checks.action: If trained: verify coil ohms dead; never force contacts live.
quick_checks.risk_if_ignored: Live tests on wet pad are shock hazards.

quick_checks.label: Capacitor label
quick_checks.action: Note MFD and voltage class for replacement spec—not guess.
quick_checks.risk_if_ignored: Wrong cap cooks fan and compressor start windings.

safe_diy: Clear ants/debris from contactor bay if visible with power locked out.
Note exact breaker behavior for the tech.
call_pro_if: Melted disconnect, hot whip, or lug discoloration.
Compressor hums, high amps, then trips.
After-hours no-cool with children or elderly heat-vulnerable occupants.

nodes.label: Line voltage at load side of disconnect
Stat, float, or low-voltage wiring repair
Capacitor, fan motor, or compressor electrical

flow_explanations.A: Confirm stat actually calling outdoor branch.
flow_explanations.B: Missing Y is often float, wire, or board lockout.
flow_explanations.D: Pitted contacts pass voltage test under load only—tech measurement.
flow_explanations.E: Low voltage and interlocks are homeowner-adjacent only with limits.
flow_explanations.F: Corrosion and storm damage concentrate here in coastal humidity.
flow_explanations.G: Start components and motors are measurable; compressor is pro-grade.

top_causes.why_it_happens: Human error, storm, or trip on fault.
Coil weak or contacts pitted; ants common.
Pan switch opens R to outdoor.
Ground, open, or start winding issue.

how_system_works: The outdoor unit needs line voltage switched by the contactor to the fan and compressor. The thermostat request closes a low-voltage path; safeties can interrupt it. Start capacitors provide phase shift torque for PSC motors.

repair_if: Localized contactor, cap, float, whip, or fan motor failure
Compressor starts reliably after documented electrical repair

local_context: Humidity and insects accelerate contactor pitting; afternoon thunderstorms cause nuisance trips.
field_insight: Outdoor silent with indoor running: I check Y before I touch the cap bucket—half are float or stat.
maintenance: Gentle contactor area cleanout with power locked out by pro
cost_pressure: Heat-index emergency pricing stacks when many homes lose outdoor after storms.
decision_moment: If breaker trips twice, stop—third is not charm; it is damage.
cta_primary: Same-day outdoor electrical diagnosis: 24 V, line voltage, contactor, and start network.
cta_secondary: Hot parts, smoke, or single loud hum—shut disconnect and call now.
stop_diy: Do not energize unit with cover off; do not pry welded contactor while live; do not sub wrong capacitor.
